title: PRECEPT Nurses Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
plain_summary: This bill creates a tax credit to help nurses who teach and train other nurses. Nurses who spend at least 200 hours per year training nursing students or new nurses in areas that don't have enough healthcare workers can get a $2,000 tax credit. The tax credit will be available from 2026 through 2032. The government will track how many nurses use this credit and report to Congress each year about whether it is working to get more nurses to become teachers.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Providing Real-World Education and Clinical Experience by Precepting Tomorrow's Nurses Act or the PRECEPT Nurses Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Credit for Nurse Preceptors
sections.text:
This section creates a new tax credit for nurse preceptors. A nurse preceptor who qualifies can get a two thousand dollar tax credit each year. To qualify, a person must be an eligible nurse preceptor, which means they must work at least two hundred hours during the tax year as a nurse preceptor in a community that the government has identified as having a shortage of health professionals. The Internal Revenue Service will publish a list of these areas on their website every year.

A nurse preceptor is a licensed registered nurse or health care provider who supervises and teaches nursing students or newly hired nurses. They provide hands-on learning, training, instruction, and mentoring in the actual practice of nursing. The teaching must follow the rules set by the State Board of Nursing, a state agency, or a written agreement between the school and the clinical site. A newly hired nurse means someone who has been working for six months or less.

To get the tax credit, the nurse preceptor must have a certification proving they completed at least two hundred hours of precepting work during the tax year. This certification must come from either the nursing school where they taught students or from their workplace where they mentored newly hired nurses. The certification must state how many hours the person worked as a preceptor. A nurse preceptor can get multiple certifications from different places to reach the two hundred hour requirement.

This tax credit will end after December thirty first, two thousand thirty two. The credit starts applying to tax years beginning after December thirty first, two thousand twenty five.

Starting with tax year two thousand twenty six and ending with tax year two thousand thirty two, the Secretary of the Treasury must report to Congress each year about this tax credit. The report must include how many taxpayers claimed the credit, the total hours served and other data about the preceptorships, where geographically the taxpayers claiming the credit are located, and other relevant information.

By June thirtieth, two thousand thirty three, the Secretary of the Treasury must work with the Administrator of the Health Resources and Services Administration to evaluate how well this tax credit worked to increase the number of nurse preceptors in the United States. They must report their findings to the relevant committees in Congress, which include the Committee on Finance of the Senate, the Committee on Ways and Means of the House of Representatives, the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions of the Senate, the Committee on Education and Workforce of the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Energy and Commerce of the House of Representatives.
